name: Pictographs and Bar Graphs — Pictures That Talk
whatIsIt: A graph turns a pile of numbers into a picture your eyes can read in one glance. A PICTOGRAPH uses symbols (🍎 = 5 fruits — check the key!); a BAR GRAPH uses bars whose HEIGHTS show the counts. Taller bar = bigger number: comparing becomes as easy as looking.
whyNeeded: Numbers in a list hide their story; the same numbers in a graph SHOUT it. Newspapers, weather reports, class surveys, cricket scorecards — the world reports itself in graphs. Reading them (and spotting when they trick you!) is everyday superpower stuff.
realLifeUses: Class survey: favourite fruits of Class 4 as a bar graph
Weather: rainy days per month on the news
Cricket: runs per over as tall and short bars
School: books read by each class this term

vocabulary.meaning: A graph using picture symbols — the KEY says what one symbol is worth.
A graph using bar heights to show counts.
The decoder: '1 symbol = 5 fruits' or 'each grid line = 2'.

story.title: The Village Fruit Vote
story.text: The class votes for the fair's fruit stall: mango 12, banana 8, apple 4. Meena draws a bar graph. 'Which fruit won, and how can you tell fastest?' asks the teacher. 'Mango — its bar towers over the others! I can see it in a single glance,' says Meena. 'The graph shows at once what the long list hid,' the teacher nods.
story.extractedProblem: Votes: mango 12, banana 8, apple 4. Which fruit won, and how can you tell fastest?
story.answerInStory: Mango — its bar towers over the others. The graph shows in a glance what the list hid.

visual.caption: Bar heights ARE the numbers: mango 12, banana 8, apple 4. Compare with your eyes, verify with the labels!

teachingGallery.title: Reading a bar graph
teachingGallery.note: Taller bar = more. Read across to the number the bar reaches.

examples.caption: Red and blue are equal (4 each).

standardMethod.summary: Read the title, read the KEY/scale, then read heights — only then compare or calculate.
steps: TITLE first: what is this graph about?
KEY/SCALE next: what is one symbol or one grid line worth?
Read each bar or symbol row into a real number.
NOW answer: tallest? shortest? how many more? total?

alternateMethods.name: Tally-to-graph (making your own)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Turning your own survey into a graph.
steps: Collect answers with tally marks (bundle the 5th across — ||||).
Count each tally group into a number.
Draw one bar per group, heights matching the numbers. Add title + key!
alternateMethods.example: Pets survey: dogs |||| ||, cats ||||, fish || → bars of 7, 4, 2.

workedExamples.problem: A pictograph shows library visits: Monday 🟦🟦🟦, Friday 🟦🟦🟦🟦🟦. Key: 🟦 = 4 students. How many came on Friday?
In the fruit graph (mango 12, banana 8, apple 4), Ravi says 'mango beat banana by 2 because the bars look 2 apart'. Check him with the numbers!
steps: Looks lie when the scale is big — read VALUES, not gaps on air.
12 − 8 = 4. Mango won by 4 votes, not 2 — the 'how many more' question is a subtraction!

trickPractice.trick: Tallest = most, read to the top
trickPractice.intro: Compare bar heights for most/least; read across from the bar's top for its value.

questions.q: In the fruit graph — mango 12, banana 8, apple 4 — which is the MOST liked?
hintLadder: The tallest bar means the most.

questions.q: In the graph, the banana bar reaches 8. How many chose banana?
hintLadder: Read across from the top of the bar.

questions.q: Mango 12 and apple 4. How many MORE chose mango?
Mango 12, banana 8, apple 4 — which is the LEAST liked?
Mango 12, banana 8, apple 4. How many children in ALL?

commonMistakes.mistake: Counting symbols as 1 each when the key says one symbol = 2, 5, or 10.
commonMistakes.fix: The key is the graph's secret decoder — read it FIRST, always. Five symbols with '1 = 4' is twenty, not five!

commonMistakes.mistake: Answering 'how many more?' by eyeballing the gap instead of subtracting the values.
commonMistakes.fix: Eyes find the tallest, but NUMBERS answer 'how many more': read both values off the scale, then subtract. 12 − 8 = 4.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the tallest bar's value when asked for the TOTAL of all bars.
commonMistakes.fix: Total means EVERYONE: add all the bars (12+8+4 = 24). The tallest bar is just the winner, not the whole story.

easy.q: In the Grove fruit graph, which fruit got the most votes?
A pictograph key says 🟦 = 4 students. Friday shows 5 symbols. How many students?

medium.q: Mango got 12 votes, banana got 8. How many MORE votes did mango get?
hintLadder: 'How many more' is a subtraction question.
Read both values from the graph.

medium.q: Fruit graph totals: mango 12 + banana 8 + apple 4. How many children voted in ALL?

challenge.q: A pictograph shows 3 symbols for Monday (key: 1 symbol = 10 books). Ravi reports 'only 3 books on Monday!' What did he skip?
hintLadder: What must you read BEFORE counting symbols?

challenge.q: Runs per over: over-1 six runs, over-2 twelve, over-3 nine. The team wants the TOTAL. What is it?
hintLadder: Total needs ALL bars, not the tallest.

questions.q: The FIRST thing to read on any pictograph:

options.label: The key — what one symbol is worth

questions.answer: The key — what one symbol is worth
hintLadder: The decoder ring of the graph.

questions.q: Bars: cats 9, dogs 15. How many more dog-lovers than cat-lovers?
Key: 1 symbol = 5 kites. Saturday shows 7 symbols. Kites sold on Saturday?
Asked for the TOTAL of all bars, you should…
hintLadder: Everyone counts, not just the winner.

teachBackPrompt: Run your own mini Newsroom: survey your family's favourite dinner (4-5 people), tally it, draw a quick bar graph, and present the 'headline' — explaining why the graph tells the story faster than your list.

revisionCard.summary: Graphs = numbers as pictures. Reading order: TITLE → KEY/scale → values → answer. Pictograph symbols are worth what the KEY says (× them!). 'How many more' = subtract values; 'total' = add ALL bars. Tallest = winner, not total!

gameMission.title: The Headline Machine
gameMission.brief: The Grove Gazette goes to print at sunset! Turn messy survey lists into bar graphs, decode sneaky pictograph keys, and fact-check Ravi's headlines before they hit the front page.

realLifeProject: Graph your week: count something for 5 days (glasses of water, pages read, minutes of play) and draw the bar graph with a title and scale. What headline does your week deserve?
